questions.stem: A topology $\mathcal{T}$ on a set $X$ is a collection of subsets of $X$ that must satisfy which of the following?
options: It is closed under arbitrary intersections and finite unions.
It contains $\emptyset$ and $X$, and is closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.
It contains only $X$ and is closed under complements.
It is closed under arbitrary unions and arbitrary intersections.
questions.explanation: By definition a topology on $X$ must contain $\emptyset$ and $X$, be closed under arbitrary (any) unions of its members, and be closed under finite intersections. The asymmetry (arbitrary unions but only finite intersections) is essential and distinguishes the axioms.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a topological space $(X, \mathcal{T})$, a set is called open precisely when it is which of the following?
options: The complement of a member of $\mathcal{T}$.
questions.explanation: The open sets are by definition exactly the members of the topology $\mathcal{T}$. A set whose complement is open is called closed, and a set equal to its own closure is closed, so those describe a different notion.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A subset $A$ of a topological space $X$ is closed when which condition holds?
options: $A$ contains none of its limit points.
questions.explanation: A set $A$ is closed exactly when its complement $X \setminus A$ is open. Equivalently, $A$ is closed iff it contains all of its limit points, but the direct definition is via the complement being open.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The discrete topology on a set $X$ is which collection of subsets?
options: All finite subsets of $X$ together with $X$.
All subsets whose complement is finite, together with $\emptyset$.
questions.explanation: The discrete topology on $X$ is the power set $\mathcal{P}(X)$: every subset is open. The collection $\{\emptyset, X\}$ is the indiscrete (trivial) topology, and the cofinite topology consists of $\emptyset$ plus the sets with finite complement.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The indiscrete (trivial) topology on a set $X$ consists of exactly which sets?
questions.explanation: The indiscrete topology is $\{\emptyset, X\}$, the smallest topology on $X$. It must contain both $\emptyset$ and $X$ to satisfy the axioms, and it contains nothing else.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the cofinite topology on an infinite set $X$, a nonempty set $U$ is open if and only if which condition holds?
questions.explanation: The cofinite topology declares $\emptyset$ open and a nonempty set $U$ open exactly when its complement $X \setminus U$ is finite. So the open sets (other than $\emptyset$) are the cofinite sets.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $\mathcal{B}$ be a basis for a topology on $X$. A subset $U$ of $X$ is open in the generated topology if and only if which holds?
options: $U$ is a finite intersection of basis elements.
$U$ is the complement of a basis element.
For each $x \in U$ there is a basis element $B$ with $x \in B \subseteq U$.
questions.explanation: In the topology generated by a basis $\mathcal{B}$, a set $U$ is open exactly when every point of $U$ lies in some basis element contained in $U$. Equivalently, every open set is a union of basis elements.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A collection $\mathcal{S}$ of subsets of $X$ is a subbasis for a topology on $X$ (with $X = \bigcup \mathcal{S}$). The topology it generates is obtained how?
options: Take all finite intersections of members of $\mathcal{S}$ to form a basis, then all unions of those.
Take all complements of members of $\mathcal{S}$.
Take only the members of $\mathcal{S}$ themselves.
Take all arbitrary intersections of members of $\mathcal{S}$.
questions.explanation: From a subbasis $\mathcal{S}$, the finite intersections of subbasis elements form a basis, and the topology consists of all unions of those basis elements. This is exactly the smallest topology containing $\mathcal{S}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The closure $\overline{A}$ of a subset $A$ of a topological space $X$ is best described as which set?
options: The union of all open sets contained in $A$.
The set of interior points of $A$.
The smallest closed set containing $A$ (the intersection of all closed sets containing $A$).
The set $A$ together with its isolated points.
questions.explanation: The closure $\overline{A}$ is the intersection of all closed sets containing $A$, equivalently the smallest closed set that contains $A$. The union of open sets contained in $A$ is the interior, a different operation.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The interior $\operatorname{int}(A)$ of a subset $A$ of $X$ is which set?
options: The largest open set contained in $A$ (the union of all open subsets of $A$).
The complement of the closure of $A$.
questions.explanation: The interior of $A$ is the union of all open sets contained in $A$, equivalently the largest open set inside $A$. The complement of the closure is the exterior, not the interior.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A point $x$ is a limit point (accumulation point) of a set $A$ in $X$ when which condition holds?
options: Every open set containing $x$ meets $A$ in some point other than $x$ itself.
Some open set containing $x$ is disjoint from $A$.
questions.explanation: $x$ is a limit point of $A$ if every neighborhood of $x$ contains a point of $A$ different from $x$. The closure satisfies $\overline{A} = A \cup A'$, where $A'$ is the set of limit points.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: On the real line $\mathbb{R}$, the standard topology is the one generated by which basis?
questions.explanation: The standard (usual) topology on $\mathbb{R}$ is generated by the basis of open intervals $(a,b)$. Using half-open intervals $[a,b)$ instead gives the lower limit topology, and singletons give the discrete topology.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X = \{a, b, c\}$ with topology $\mathcal{T} = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a,b\}, X\}$. Which set is closed in $X$?
questions.explanation: The closed sets are the complements of the open sets: complements of $\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a,b\}, X$ are $X, \{b,c\}, \{c\}, \emptyset$. So $\{b,c\}$ is closed, while $\{a\}$ and $\{a,b\}$ are open (not closed) and $\{b\}$ is neither.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X = \{a, b, c\}$ with topology $\mathcal{T} = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a,b\}, X\}$. What is the closure of $\{a\}$?
questions.explanation: The closed sets are $X, \{b,c\}, \{c\}, \emptyset$. The closure of $\{a\}$ is the smallest closed set containing $a$. Among the closed sets, only $X$ contains $a$, so $\overline{\{a\}} = X$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: How many distinct topologies can be defined on a two-point set $X = \{a, b\}$?
questions.explanation: Every topology must contain $\emptyset$ and $X$. The remaining choice is which of $\{a\}, \{b\}$ to include: neither, just $\{a\}$, just $\{b\}$, or both. That gives $4$ topologies on a two-point set.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the topology $\mathcal{T} = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a,b\}, X\}$ on $X = \{a,b,c\}$, what is the interior of $\{a, b\}$?
questions.explanation: The interior of $\{a,b\}$ is the largest open set contained in it. Since $\{a,b\}$ is itself open (it is in $\mathcal{T}$), its interior is $\{a,b\}$ itself.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Given two topologies $\mathcal{T}_1$ and $\mathcal{T}_2$ on $X$ with $\mathcal{T}_1 \subseteq \mathcal{T}_2$, we say $\mathcal{T}_2$ is which of the following relative to $\mathcal{T}_1$?
questions.explanation: If $\mathcal{T}_1 \subseteq \mathcal{T}_2$ then $\mathcal{T}_2$ has more open sets, so it is finer (stronger) and $\mathcal{T}_1$ is coarser (weaker). The discrete topology is the finest and the indiscrete is the coarsest on any set.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which statement about the closure operation is true for all subsets $A, B$ of a space $X$?
questions.explanation: Closure distributes over finite unions: $\overline{A \cup B} = \overline{A} \cup \overline{B}$. For intersections only $\overline{A \cap B} \subseteq \overline{A} \cap \overline{B}$ holds in general, the closure is always closed (not open in general), and $A \subseteq \overline{A}$, not the reverse.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the standard topology on $\mathbb{R}$, what is the closure of the open interval $(0, 1)$?
questions.explanation: The closure of $(0,1)$ adds its limit points $0$ and $1$, giving the closed interval $[0,1]$. Both endpoints are limit points since every neighborhood of $0$ or $1$ meets $(0,1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the standard topology on $\mathbb{R}$, what is the closure of the set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers?
questions.explanation: The rationals are dense in $\mathbb{R}$: every open interval contains a rational, so every real number is a limit point of $\mathbb{Q}$. Hence $\overline{\mathbb{Q}} = \mathbb{R}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the discrete topology on a set $X$, which statement about an arbitrary subset $A \subseteq X$ is correct?
options: $A$ is open but generally not closed.
$A$ is closed but generally not open.
questions.explanation: In the discrete topology every subset is open. Since complements of subsets are also subsets, every subset is also closed. Thus every subset is clopen (both open and closed).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X = \{1, 2, 3\}$. Is the collection $\mathcal{T} = \{\emptyset, \{1\}, \{2\}, \{1, 2, 3\}\}$ a topology on $X$?
options: Yes, it satisfies all the axioms.
No, because it does not contain $\emptyset$.
No, because $X$ is not in it.
No, because it fails closure under union ($\{1\} \cup \{2\} = \{1,2\}$ is missing).
questions.explanation: The union $\{1\} \cup \{2\} = \{1,2\}$ must be open for $\mathcal{T}$ to be a topology, but $\{1,2\}$ is not listed. So $\mathcal{T}$ is not closed under unions and is not a topology. (It does contain $\emptyset$ and $X$, so those are not the issue.)
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The boundary $\partial A$ of a set $A$ in a space $X$ is defined as which set?
questions.explanation: The boundary is $\partial A = \overline{A} \setminus \operatorname{int}(A)$, equivalently $\overline{A} \cap \overline{X \setminus A}$: the points in the closure of $A$ that are not interior to $A$. These are the points every neighborhood of which meets both $A$ and its complement.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the standard topology on $\mathbb{R}$, what is the interior of the closed interval $[0, 1]$?
questions.explanation: The interior is the largest open set inside $[0,1]$. The endpoints $0$ and $1$ have no neighborhood contained in $[0,1]$, so they are excluded; every point of $(0,1)$ is interior. Hence $\operatorname{int}([0,1]) = (0,1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Work on the three-point set $X = \{a, b, c\}$ with topology $\mathcal{T} = \{\emptyset, \{a\}, \{a, b\}, X\}$. Count the open sets and the closed sets, and find the closure of $\{b\}$.
questions.solution: The topology lists its open sets explicitly, so the number of open sets is {{1}}. The closed sets are the complements of the open sets, and since complementation is a bijection there are the same number of closed sets, namely {{2}}. The closed sets are $X, \{b,c\}, \{c\}, \emptyset$; the smallest one containing $b$ is $\{b,c\}$, so the closure of $\{b\}$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: There are $4$ open sets as listed. Complementation pairs them with $4$ closed sets $X, \{b,c\}, \{c\}, \emptyset$. The smallest closed set containing $b$ is $\{b,c\}$, so $\overline{\{b\}} = \{b,c\}$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Count topologies on small sets. Then classify the trivial topology by fineness.
questions.solution: On a one-point set $X = \{a\}$ the only collection containing $\emptyset$ and $X$ is $\{\emptyset, X\}$, so the number of distinct topologies is {{1}}. On a two-point set the count rises to {{2}}. On any set, the indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, X\}$ is the {{3}} topology, having the fewest open sets.
questions.explanation: A one-point set admits exactly $1$ topology. A two-point set admits $4$ (choose any subcollection of $\{a\},\{b\}$ to adjoin to $\emptyset, X$). The indiscrete topology is the coarsest (smallest) topology on any set.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Work in the standard topology on $\mathbb{R}$. Consider the set $A = (0, 1) \cup \{2\}$. Find its closure and decide whether $2$ is a limit point of $A$.
questions.solution: The closure of $(0,1)$ is $[0,1]$, and the isolated point $2$ is already closed, so $\overline{A} = [0,1] \cup \{2\}$, which contains $[0,1]$ plus the single extra point. The total number of isolated points of $A$ in its closure is {{1}}, namely the point $2$. Since no neighborhood of $2$ meets $A$ in a point other than $2$, the point $2$ {{2}} a limit point of $A$. The number of limit points of $A$ that lie in the interval $[0,1]$ is {{3}} (all of $[0,1]$).
questions.explanation: The closure is $[0,1] \cup \{2\}$. The point $2$ is isolated (a small interval around it misses $(0,1)$), so it is not a limit point; there is exactly $1$ such isolated point. Every point of $[0,1]$ is a limit point of $A$, so there are infinitely many limit points in $[0,1]$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X = \{a, b, c\}$ carry the discrete topology. Count its open sets, and classify each subset as open, closed, or clopen.
questions.solution: The discrete topology is the full power set, so the number of open sets equals the number of subsets of a three-element set, which is {{1}}. Because complements of subsets are again subsets, every subset is both open and closed, that is {{2}}. In particular the singleton $\{a\}$ is {{3}} in this topology.
questions.explanation: A three-element set has $2^3 = 8$ subsets, all open in the discrete topology. Every subset is also closed (its complement is open), so every subset, including $\{a\}$, is clopen.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
